speaker: interviewer
text: Number four interview with Zalman Finkelstein, August 15, 1996. How long were you in Dachau [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with this machinery?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too long. They took me out. They took out, they closed it off. They closed this whole place off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I think was a bomb in it. An American or Russian bomb was down there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. You mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: In [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: The place was bombed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you there when it was bombed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We were not there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- I think was a-- a-- a-- a attack. I think it's later. Because when I left was halfway work, was not work enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. You were in Dachau, oh, till about-- till the middle of 1944. And then you went to where, to--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Lager--

speaker: interviewer
text: --to Kaufering?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kaufering. Lager-- Lager I and Lager IV, this what I was. These are numbers. Lager I and Lager IV.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- where is Kaufering in relation to Dachau, how did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they're not-- they're not far.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there, to Kaufering?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think by walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the difference between Lager I and Lager IV?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Lager I and Lager IV, they are just-- one-- one Lager was I was workin' they build a new, I believe, a new-- a new building. It was not a build, what I-- mentioned, a building for airplanes, for manufacturing. And there-- there was a-- was something like this, a hole down there, I don't know-- 20 stories deep in the ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was below ground.

speaker: interviewee
text: Below ground. The-- above the ground was just like a round-- a round-- quite high, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And were you to build or to work in the factor?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, nothing to work yet. What they-- they build it. They build it. I think that this was a company, [? Mool, ?] that they're producing airplanes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: M-O-O-L. This what I think, [? Mool, ?] [? Mool. ?] I don't know if I pronounce it right or whatever it is. And they build this and they-- they used to have-- the roof was not finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: This whole-- whole gigantic. This was, you know, by foot. This was maybe 8 feet thick.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your job there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- over there was hundreds of Jewish people working, was just iron. You know, they put in concrete iron. And they tied it down, and they put in this thick iron, you know, a lot. You know? The whole-- this was long that the airplane can go in and-- and this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --when it's finished. So we used to stay and from the bottom up, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] train with-- with the cement, with everything the Germans had technique with cement. And they used to go in 8 inches pipes, the-- the concrete up by force. And the Jewish people were standing with the sticks. I was standing with long sticks that fills up no empty-- no holes in the concrete.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, people used to slide and fall in. They used to cover them up [? right there. ?] Was nobody in [? trap. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was going like water, the concrete.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was when you were in Lager I or Lager IV?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or in both? What was the difference between the Lager I and Lager IV?

speaker: interviewee
text: Over there I was working with-- with the machine, here I was working with the-- with the-- with the--

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pushing the concrete in.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was till the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Right till the end?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: You stayed till the end?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: You just built. Did you see the airplanes at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Nothing there yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the plane.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were just building the building.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because you-- we saw the Americans, we saw the Russians. You know, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw the planes or the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw the planes. Because when-- at-- every time was an alarm they used to chase out from this place. Because down there, the Germans used to run in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to hide from the bombs. And-- and us they used to send out on the field. So we used to sit on the field. And-- and we used to see the-- the things that they throw down, give up and all kinds of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean the leaflets, the pamphlets?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Give up, you're going to [? be ?] [? destroyed. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the barracks like in Kaufering, the same as in Dachau or different?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same thing, on [INAUDIBLE]. On-- on this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food? You-- you went out to work, you came back, outside was a little soup. And sometime they give you a piece of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever get sick there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever punished?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have the same Blockalteste there, too, or kapo?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this-- this-- this kapo was-- this-- no, another one.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did people--

speaker: interviewee
text: But I-- I didn't finish this, you know. I saved this guy from the truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the--

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't go to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the-- oh, I thought you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not. Take a look, my skin is wrinkled. You see, it's wrinkled. I go back. Take a look.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean the-- in-- the-- from the [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. I thought you had finished [INAUDIBLE]. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I didn't leave him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't leave him. As the truck-- you know, they stood near the-- the-- the-- the-- the-- where we slept and everything. I-- I went up from the line between the five people--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and reached the truck. And I-- and I-- and I-- start telling him, Moishe, Moishe, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] [? to the ?] end, you know, by the truck. And I climbed a little bit in, and I throw him down on the floor and schlepped him into barrack and put him where the [? Blockalteste ?] is laying-- [LAUGHS].

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And covered him up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And no one saw this?

speaker: interviewee
text: And nobody saw it. A miracle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did he survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: He survived. He is in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was crazy anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was Kaufering?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I wasn't afraid for anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was Dachau or Kaufering? Kaufering.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this-- this-- this was Kaufering, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kaufering. What did people talk about it after, when they came back from work? What did you talk about in the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We were sitting on the edge, sitting on the edge. Take off this [NON-ENGLISH], this one thing and take the lice out, squeeze it. They would dig like a-- a quarter inch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Finkelstein, what kept you going in these camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. I have no idea. I have no idea. I was like everybody, a bull, just-- just thinking for food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people-- did people daven, did they pray?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Moishe, but I'm talking about the second one, Moishe [NON-ENGLISH]. His father was a big rabbi and very religious, this boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he prayed.

speaker: interviewee
text: When he came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know in there what the-- what the calendar was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: When holidays were?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that the war was sort of coming to an end?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know about what was going on in the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] After Kaufering, you said you were moved to Dachau again from Kaufering.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm a little confused with this, if this was straight from the-- from Lager I. But I'm positive that I was back, they took back. And then from there, they build up the group of people for the march. So I'm a little confused because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how you were called to go to the march?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Everybody, like you come up in the morning, and you stay in your-- from your block.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: You stay in it. And then we went out. The-- the-- the-- each time they went out from the camp and they put you on the road. And you know, and-- and then-- then the German came with the wagons, pushing-- the guys were pushing their food, the ammunition. And every 50 feet or whatever was soldiers on each side watching with dogs and everything. And we start marching. You heard shooting once in a while. After a few days walking, people couldn't walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they feed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They give you nothing. Nothing. I don't remember ever getting-- get anything there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you were there in a row?

speaker: interviewer
text: In a-- in one row?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. Five, five in a row.

speaker: interviewer
text: Five. And did people-- did people see you marching? Did you go through villages?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Sure. [? We go-- ?] they gonna the-- the ways they picked, they picked not main roads. They picked through farms, little towns. And people stood in the windows and the doors and looking to us. I remember, oh my God, so God help me. I-- I-- we passed by a bakery and the bread smell, oh my God. This was-- [SIGHS] oh, we take-- and the people were watching. They stood in the door there, you know, 50 feet away.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they saw people falling down and-- and there the Germans screaming and shooting.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you march?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stop to sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. We were sitting down probably somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this what it is-- on the side.

speaker: interviewer
text: You marched at night.

speaker: interviewee
text: True. Was no houses, no nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you march to?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Their plan was-- was not far already. Their plan was I think 10 days we've marched. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They-- they had the plan. It was a ditch, a natural ditch, mountains around it, mountains. And was deep, deep. And this was their plan. This was mined all the way around to empty out the camp with the people not to leave to them. Because they saw the Americans and Russians, they're coming closer. So this was their plan not to give in. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So all of a sudden-- all of a sudden, this would happen. All of a sudden, you saw that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] less soldiers. Instead you no have 100 or 50. Was no soldiers. Was one soldier on this side, then another 100 feet or 200 feet another solider. And the alte kakers, you know, those-- it is. So those people, I don't know if they got an order or something. We passed by-- our group, we passed by farms. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So they start breaking up. And they put us in-- in the-- in the-- what do you call this, where they keep the hay? Barns.

speaker: interviewee
text: A barn. So they put us up in a barn. A bigger barn, more people; less barn, less people. So we were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 40 people, 50 people in the barn. And inside was a wagon what they carry the hay-- you know, those open wagons? And they stood in there, and we were inside. No bread, no water, no nothing. We were there a few hours. And then a guy came with a-- with a pail and water and a glass. And he gave us water.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the guy?

speaker: interviewee
text: That guy was the farmer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing. Nothing. And he gave-- he gave us water. I mean, everybody had a drink of water. And then we saw there's only one soldier watches us, only one soldier. He sits by the door, [GERMAN], sits by the door with a big [GERMAN]. He falls asleep. [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was there a few days. So I had a few guys with me. And I said, you know something? We're going to finish him off. We're going to go out.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, we broke-- I-- on the wagon, I went up on the wagon. Another two guys went up with me. And we-- we poke a little at the wood, you know, from the-- these fields, fields with hay, with this, with that-- fields. [BACKGROUND NOISE] I said, let's go out through here. We're gonna take up [INAUDIBLE] a boat. And so it happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: You ran away.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Didn't run away. We were two guys and two guys [? were there. ?] If I'm not success, you jump in. So we took the sticks from this wagon that hold it, took apart. And we went around this when he's sleeping and the other guy around this. If I miss, you go, you hit. And we killed him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we ran out. And in the field was [NON-ENGLISH]. To bring water up there. But anyway, that's how I remember it.

speaker: interviewer
text: A well, you mean.

speaker: interviewee
text: A well. And we behind the whole thing in the field, we all 50-- or 45, I don't know-- were laying a whole night in the field, a whole day in the field. And next day, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we saw-- we're laying still, not eating, no-- no we [? chew-- ?] the-- the [INAUDIBLE] [LAUGHS] was going there. And-- and we saw the farmer looking for us. So he didn't know, he was stupid. He didn't know if we went away. We hide the guy, the-- the policeman, this-- this soldier. You know, we pulled him with us into the field. So he probably thought that we all went with him away our barrack. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So two days later in the-- early in the morning, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we see a Jeep. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't know Americans. I didn't never saw American. And so [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jeep-- the driver sits in the Jeep. And the guy with the machine gun in the side sits in the back ready with the machine gun. I didn't know these Americans, I didn't know. But he goes and he turns. And they're going slowly because each time they're going in, they're afraid that they're being attacked, you know. They just-- they went forward, you know. So a Jeep. 10 minutes later, I see a command car with a big machine in the middle. And you have four guys already, two with a machine gun, looking, quiet. 10 minutes later, I see a few tanks coming. Then they're marching the tanks-- and a lot of tanks. And they stopped. The whole convoy, you know, stopped. And soldiers coming out and start smoking, talking. And we're laying there. We're afraid. Maybe they're going

speaker: interviewer
text: We didn't know. We didn't know nothing. So we start with the-- we start making a little noise. Oy. Oy. [LAUGHS] Oy. Like this. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one guy, two guys, they saw the hay moves. They told us, how did you find us there? You know. First we couldn't talk, couldn't talk. Talked with the hands. So he-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from the road there, a dirt road. He speak in English, but we couldn't get it. You didn't understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: Till he-- he said later on to stand up and with the hand up. They didn't know who was there. But when we bend up a little more, you know, he was-- they were shooting in the-- in the air. It scare us. So he saw that this is-- this is not Germans with the [NON-ENGLISH]. Because he passed already-- they passed already--

speaker: interviewer
text: The striped uniforms.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. A guy in the back throw a hat to a few guys, you know, the [INAUDIBLE] who had the hat. You know, he throw away the hat. So they saw that this is not Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they said, up, everybody up. Then right away, you know, come trucks. Right away. Not just this, the guy that I was close, talking to him, he took out a block of chocolate and gave me to eat the chocolate. They were Americans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Americans, yeah. Gave-- everybody gave somebody-- [? black guys ?] were good chauffeurs. [INAUDIBLE] they were driving the trucks, blacks.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you able to communicate with the Americans? Was there anyone that spoke Polish or Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had one guy that he was Jewish, one guy--

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: --in the hospital. He brought another transportation-- another transport with people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. But you knew they were Americans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Later I--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you knew you were free.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: We saw already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were-- where was this? Do you know what town this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was Bad Tolz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bad Tolz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bad Tolz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was really a farm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if I'm-- I'm doing right with the name of it, but this was Bad Tolz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fine. Fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think. [LAUGHS] This was Bad Tolz and I knew we were free. They said, too. OK. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did everybody do--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would-- what did everyone do when you realized that you had been liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I think you start living. You come back--

speaker: interviewer
text: But what did you do personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: You-- you were-- you were crazy, you know? You-- you-- you didn't think much because nothing was there. But your movements and things-- the smile of your face was not a smile but was a cry, but a happy cry. I'm crying now, a happy cry. And this what was. You had-- was no had, was no had. You were a plain dumb person, plain dumb-- nothing. You licked from the floor, you-- you-- you eat worms, you eat grass. I ate grass. I had a grass that was delicious, sweet as sugar.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when they-- the Americans were already there, what did you and your-- your friends or your people that were your-- with you in the hay, what did you all say to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you say anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were going like-- like-- like sheeps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And where did they take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The trucks that took us to the hospital, where the hospital is, [YIDDISH]. I don't know where. We got-- if this was a crematorium, you go to the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know you were free?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew you were free.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They-- they-- they were clapping on your back, helping you walking into the truck, helping you up to the truck, you know? They put down things that you can walk up, human. So-- so it was good. You saw. They put you in the hospital. Oh, no. You saw that you're going from bad and not for worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Where-- do you know where the hospital was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right in this town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And how long were you in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few days. I was-- I was good. I was walking, just not eating. They didn't gave you food. They gave you just cereal a little bit. Thousands of people, even from this group, thousands died-- thousands died of dysentery. Dysentery, how you say it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Dysentery.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dysentery. They died. They couldn't eat, and they came in and ate everything, fill it up. And they died, destroyed-- thousands, thousands.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do after that, after you-- they released you from the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was walking with another guy. I was walking by foot till Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: A nice few miles. I don't know-- 40, 50. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was walking to Munich to go to the camps there to see if it's-- because they start making names and a shield on the outside to see who was in the camp, your names. We start looking--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody help you, going to Munich?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: You just walked.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you reconnect with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have-- I didn't-- oh, how I did connect. In Germany, I didn't have nobody. I didn't find anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what had happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened is I was going-- I was in a few camps where they keep the people. The most was Munich, headquarters. And they had a list there. So I-- I-- I-- I knew who was alive and who was dead. I knew my sister maybe survived, maybe. And I knew my brother is in Russia. That's all. The rest, nothing. And me. So what I-- I just looked, actually, for my sister. Or I look just for Finkelstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you find her?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] When I went to Italy, I saw I have nobody there. I couldn't stay there, I didn't want to stay there no more.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to Italy. How did you get to Italy?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Italy, they picked in this camp over there, they picked a group that they can do things.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which camp, in the Munich, you're talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Munich, that they can do the-- they are responsible, they are knowledgeable. They ask you what-- what-- where you come from, who's your father, what did you do. So that-- so they picked me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they ask you, what is your ideas? Where you want to go? Where do you wanna to live, you know? I said, I want to go to Israel. And Israel was not Israel yet. No, it was Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Palestine. But I-- I-- even in Poland, I was a little-- a chalutz already when I joined the organization and everything. So I had in mind something like that. I want to live in a commune. So this was in my mind. So I have nobody. I went-- they picked a group of people, about 30 people, 35. Was-- we had three girls in it, and the rest was boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who picked?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe this was people from Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this Bricha?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Bricha, yeah. For people-- the Bricha [? over ?] the Haganah, they-- they were together but it's two different--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a Jewish agency?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not Jewish agency. This was involved already to do some work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know yet nothing. But we said that the plan is, I want to live in Israel. So they had me-- so we had-- they picked from a few thousand people this amount. And we went to Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: American trucks. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] American trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in-- where in Italy did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- they dropped us off in Santa Maria. This was the town, a small town on the ocean. Beautiful. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they put us in in a house, alone, a private house. I think if it's right that I remember right that this was a German, a big, rich millionaire. He was living on this mountain. And the water's down there, beautiful all the way around with the grapefruit-- not grapefruit--

speaker: interviewer

text: The-- I'm a [? little out. ?]

text: Little ones?

text: No, not little ones. Not grapefruit.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what it is. Grapefruit, yes. So around there in a big lake [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and the ocean on this side. And what they did is I think they said, you're gonna get food. You have to cook for yourself, and we're gonna talk to you later. Don't worry, everything is OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there came people, I don't know who they are. This was somebody generally, I don't know, from the Jewish-- from Jewish philanthropies or whatever. Some delegates came, and they said, listen, don't worry, it's in good hands. We're going to supply you with food.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're going to get everything. Cook and eat and that's it. And some other people are gonna see you. So the other people were two people that the-- from the Haganah or whatever. And they said, you people have a mission. You have a mission.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who is a good swimmer? Who can speak some language extra? So those people we start off with about six, seven people that [INAUDIBLE]. So I was between them. And what happened is we start working to bring the aliyah to Israel. How?

speaker: interviewee
text: So now, this house was not far that you can go into the water. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So you needed a--

speaker: interviewer

speaker: interviewee
text: --a boat or something. And this house was good. The boat could go underneath the house, you know, it had the bridge over it. And this owner, the guy who had, was placed space for three cars-- for three boats.

speaker: interviewer
